verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/families/gaussian.py:108 "return safe_kl_clamp(div, kl_max=kl_max)" (with eps=1e-6 at L73; KL branch L87 "div = 0.5 * (trace_term + mahal_term - K + logdet_term)" summed via .sum(dim=-1); Renyi alpha>1 NaN mask L106-107 "ok = (raw_blend > 0.0).all(dim=-1) ... torch.where(ok, div, ...nan)")
verdict.evidence_v2: _numerics.py:256 diag_kl "return 0.5 * (_sq/_sp + (mu_q-mu_p)**2/_sp - 1.0 + _sp.log() - _sq.log())" (eps=1e-8 at L229, per-dim, NO clamp, NO sum); _numerics.py:285 "(unclamped, mirroring diag_kl)"; clamp applied by caller e_step.py:653 "...self.alpha_divergence, eps=eps,).clamp(min=0.0)"
verdict.finding: The load-bearing inner arithmetic IS identical in the pure alpha in (0,1] regime, so the claim's core technical assertion is correct and golden parity can hold within the common window. But "same / only packaging differs" overstates it. V3 renyi_closed_form is NOT a drop-in equivalent of V2 diag_kl/diag_renyi: it bundles three extra operations V2's primitives deliberately omit — (1) reduction over the last dim plus the -K form (vs V2's unclamped per-dim -1.0 tensor that the caller sums); (2) safe_kl_clamp(kl_max=100.0) with NaN/+inf->100 and -inf->0 mapping (V2's functions are explicitly "unclamped"; V2 clamps separately via .clamp(min=0.0) at e_step.py:653, with NO 100-cap); (3) an alpha>1 all-dim NaN gate (ok = (raw_blend>0).all(dim=-1)) absent from V2 diag_renyi. The eps default also differs: V3 1e-6 vs V2 1e-8 (though V2 live callers often pass eps=1e-6 explicitly). The true per-dim analog of V2's primitives is V3 renyi_per_coord, not renyi_closed_form, so the cited V3<->V2 pairing is mismatched in scope. Consequences: outside [0, kl_max] the two diverge (a divergence that should be 150 returns 150 in V2's summed path but is capped to 100 in V3); for non-PD blends at alpha>1, V3 gates whole pairs/coords to kl_max while V2 emits a finite (wrong) value or relies on the caller's min-clamp only. Verdict: math ported faithfully but repackaged with a different clamp/reduction/NaN-gating contract — "refactored," not "same."

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Family abstraction: parameter-object ABC vs covariance bool
verifiedHighPoints.v3: families/base.py:48-117 BeliefParams ABC (natural/log_partition_at/entropy/expected_statistic/block/stack); gaussian.py:19 @register_family('gaussian_diagonal') DiagonalGaussian, gaussian.py:149 @register_family('gaussian_full') FullGaussian; cov_kind ClassVar is 'the single source of truth' (base.py:67, replacing name sniffing)
verifiedHighPoints.v2: config.py:350 `diagonal_covariance: bool = True` ('True=(B,N,K), False=(B,N,K,K)'); e_step.py:382 self.diagonal_covariance read; divergences are dimension-sniffed at runtime (e_step.py:636 `if sigma_q.dim()==4: torch.diagonal(...)`), not dispatched on a family object
verifiedHighPoints.implication: V3 makes the exponential family a registered first-class object so a new family (e.g. a non-Gaussian member) slots in behind the seam by subclassing+registering; V2's family is a single boolean toggle with shape-sniffing scattered across call sites. The cov_kind ClassVar removes V2's fragile dim()==4 / name-sniffing dispatch.

verdict.topic: Family abstraction: parameter-object ABC vs covariance bool
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/families/base.py:48 "class BeliefParams(ABC):"; base.py:67 "cov_kind: ClassVar[str]"; base.py:123 "def register_family(name: str)"; base.py:138 "def family_cov_kind(name: str): ... return get_family(name).cov_kind"; vfe3/families/gaussian.py:19 "@register_family(\"gaussian_diagonal\")" / :27 "cov_kind = \"diagonal\""; gaussian.py:149 "@register_family(\"gaussian_full\")" / :156 "cov_kind = \"full\"". Abstract math interface present: natural(), log_partition_at(), entropy(), block(), broadcast_over_keys(); plus stack()/expected_statistic() hooks.
verdict.evidence_v2: config.py:350 "diagonal_covariance: bool        = True    # True = (B,N,K), False = (B,N,K,K)"; e_step.py:382 "self.diagonal_covariance = cfg.diagonal_covariance"; e_step.py:636 "if sigma_q.dim() == 4: sigma_q = torch.diagonal(sigma_q, dim1=-2, dim2=-1)". Grep over entire V2 tree: register_family/get_family/cov_kind/family_cov_kind -> "No matches found"; no Family/Belief/Gaussian/Distribution/ExpFamily ABC. The sole BeliefBundle (semantic_clustering/bundle.py:10) is a flat clustering-diagnostic dataclass with its own "diagonal: bool" field and "(n,K)" vs "(n,K,K)" shape comment, not an exp-family math object. The ".dim()==4" shape-sniffing pattern occurs 28 times across 10 files (e_step.py:10).
verdict.finding: Original relationship survives. Cited V3 paths were under vfe3/families/ (not families/), but every cited symbol exists at essentially the stated lines: BeliefParams ABC (base.py:48), cov_kind ClassVar single-source-of-truth (base.py:67, "replacing name sniffing" at base.py:53), and the two @register_family Gaussians (gaussian.py:19, 149). V2 has no counterpart: zero hits for register_family/get_family/cov_kind/family_cov_kind anywhere in the tree, no exponential-family ABC, only a config bool diagonal_covariance (config.py:350) read into e_step (382) and dispatched via runtime dim()==4 shape-sniffing (636), a pattern that recurs 28x across 10 files. The one near-miss, BeliefBundle (semantic_clustering/bundle.py), is a downstream detached-tensor clustering container carrying a diagonal:bool field and no family math/registry/divergence methods, so it reinforces rather than refutes the V2 boolean-toggle characterization. Adversarial attack found no equivalence, no renamed/relocated symbol on the V2 side, and no misread on the V3 side. The difference is genuine and high-significance: V3 makes the exponential family a registered first-class object behind a seam; V2 is a single boolean with scattered shape-sniffing.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Full-covariance Gaussian divergence: closed-form family vs not-wired autograd
verifiedHighPoints.v3: families/gaussian.py:204-271 FullGaussian.renyi_closed_form: full Cholesky-based KL (trace/mahal/logdet via safe_cholesky, lines 231-243) AND alpha!=1 blend with per-element PD masking (lines 252-270); natural/log_partition_at (178-193) enable the generic path too
verifiedHighPoints.v2: diag_renyi/diag_kl are diagonal-only (_numerics.py:224,259); the full-cov self-KL via the autograd path is explicitly NotImplementedError at e_step.py:2544-2552 ('full-cov self-KL via autograd needs logdet through eigendecomposition, not yet wired')
verifiedHighPoints.implication: V3 has a genuine, complete closed-form full-Gaussian divergence as a registered family member; the equivalent V2 path raises NotImplementedError. This is a real capability V3 adds, not a refactor — V2's full-cov support is partial and routed through other (non-divergence-primitive) machinery.

verdict.topic: Full-covariance Gaussian divergence: closed-form family vs not-wired autograd
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/families/gaussian.py:149 "@register_family(\"gaussian_full\")"; :204-243 renyi_closed_form alpha=1 full-cov KL via safe_cholesky with trace_term(:235), mahal_term(:240), logdet_p/q(:241-243); :252-270 alpha!=1 blend branch with per-element PD mask ("ok = ok_blend & ok_q & ok_t"); reachable via base.py:236-238 "closed = getattr(q, \"renyi_closed_form\", None) ... return closed(p, alpha=alpha, ...)"
verdict.evidence_v2: _numerics.py:231 "Per-dimension standard KL(q||p) for diagonal Gaussians" / :267 "Per-dimension Renyi-alpha divergence for diagonal Gaussians"; e_step.py:2547-2552 raise NotImplementedError "full-cov self-KL via autograd needs logdet through eigendecomposition; not yet wired"; e_step.py:2075 "Diagonal covariance only — full-cov KL(q||p) requires log|det| and is skipped here"
verdict.finding: The relationship holds. V3 has a genuine, registered closed-form full-covariance Gaussian divergence: FullGaussian (register_family("gaussian_full")) implements renyi_closed_form with the alpha=1 Cholesky KL (trace/mahal/logdet, gaussian.py:231-243) AND the alpha!=1 Renyi blend with per-element PD masking (gaussian.py:252-270), and base.py:236-239 routes the registry functional renyi/kl through it, so it is wired into the divergence seam. V2's diag_kl/diag_renyi (_numerics.py:224,259) are per-dimension diagonal-only, and the full-cov self-KL via autograd is NotImplementedError (e_step.py:2547-2552), exactly as cited. The adversarial attack surfaced one refinement the implication should carry but does not invalidate the claim: V2 is NOT entirely devoid of a closed-form full-cov KL — prior_bank.py:644-740 _decode_exact_full_cov computes an exact block-diagonal full-cov KL with Cholesky inverse, logdet (737-739), trace and mahalanobis terms (docstring 662-664), gated behind exact_full_cov_decode (prior_bank.py:111). However this V2 routine is (a) a DECODE-side KL of beliefs against vocab priors, not a divergence primitive usable for self/belief coupling; (b) block-diagonal per-irrep, not a general dense full-cov divergence; and (c) alpha=1 KL only — it has no full-cov Renyi (alpha!=1) counterpart, whereas V3's gaussian.py:252-270 does. So V2's full-cov KL support is partial and lives outside the divergence-primitive registry, while V3's is a complete registered family member including the Renyi blend. The claim's word "complete" must be read as scoped to the divergence-primitive seam and to alpha!=1; under that scope the divergent relationship is accurate. The docstring note that V3's renyi_closed_form was "ported verbatim from divergence._gaussian_full_renyi" refers to the V3 module vfe3/divergence.py, not a V2 source, so it does not establish any V2 equivalent.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Alpha self-coupling: registry of forms vs E_learnable_alpha bool
verifiedHighPoints.v3: alpha_i.py:19-46 register_alpha/get_alpha/alpha_is_per_coord registry; forms 'constant' (60-69), 'state_dependent' (72-84), 'learnable' (87-113), 'state_dependent_per_coord' (116-135); dispatched by config alpha_mode via self_coupling_alpha(...,mode=...) at alpha_i.py:166-181
verifiedHighPoints.v2: config.py:325 `E_learnable_alpha: bool = True`; e_step.py:626-627 `if not self.E_learnable_alpha: return self.alpha` (constant) else c0/(b0+div_k) at e_step.py:660. Exactly two states (constant scalar vs Bayesian per-dim), selected by one bool inside a method
verifiedHighPoints.implication: V3 turns V2's two-valued bool into an open registry with four registered schedules and a per_coord routing flag, extensible by registration. The 'constant' and 'state_dependent' forms reproduce V2's two branches (c0/(b0+D) matches e_step.py:660), so the existing behavior is preserved while the seam generalizes.

verdict.topic: Alpha self-coupling: registry of forms vs E_learnable_alpha bool
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/alpha_i.py:15-19 `_ALPHAS: Dict[str, Callable] = {}` ... `def register_alpha(name: str, *, per_coord: bool = False)`; four registered forms — alpha_i.py:60 `@register_alpha("constant")`, :72 `@register_alpha("state_dependent")`, :87 `@register_alpha("learnable")`, :116 `@register_alpha("state_dependent_per_coord", per_coord=True)` — dispatched at :181 `return get_alpha(mode)(kl, **kwargs)`.
verdict.evidence_v2: VFE_2.0/transformer/vfe/config.py:325 `E_learnable_alpha: bool = True`; VFE_2.0/transformer/vfe/e_step.py:626-627 `if not self.E_learnable_alpha:` / `return self.alpha`, else e_step.py:660 `return c0 / (b0 + div_k)` with div_k shape (B,N,K) per e_step.py:658.
verdict.finding: Attack failed; relationship is genuinely divergent (high significance), not refactored. (1) The refutation routes I tried all dead-end: a repo-wide grep of V2 for alpha selectors returns NO `alpha_mode`, NO `register_alpha`/registry, NO `log_alpha`/`exp(log_alpha)` form anywhere — only `E_learnable_alpha` (a bool) and `alpha_divergence` (the Renyi order, a different knob). V2's entire alpha selection is one bool inside one method `get_bayesian_alpha` (e_step.py:626-627 vs 660). So the V3 symbols `register_alpha`/`get_alpha`/`alpha_is_per_coord`/`self_coupling_alpha(mode=)` and the `learnable`/`state_dependent` forms have no V2 counterpart under any name. (2) Why it is NOT merely 'refactored' (a behavior-preserving reshuffle): V3 adds registered, selectable forms with zero V2 equivalent — `learnable` (alpha=exp(log_alpha), a free NN-exception scalar, alpha_i.py:87-113) and per-position `state_dependent` (summed self-divergence, alpha_i.py:72-84). V2 has neither. V3's behavior set is a strict superset plus an open registration seam, which is divergent, not a 1:1 restructure. (3) One correction to the claim's IMPLICATION (not the relationship): it says V3 `state_dependent` (c0/(b0+D), per-position) matches e_step.py:660. That is mislabeled — V2's adaptive branch is per-COORDINATE: `div_k` is (B,N,K) (e_step.py:658) and the self-term is `(alpha_attached * div_qp_per_dim).sum()` = sum_k alpha^(k) D^(k) (e_step.py:798). The V3 form that actually reproduces V2's c0/(b0+div_k) is `state_dependent_per_coord` (per_coord=True, contract `sum_k alpha^(k) D^(k)`, alpha_i.py:116-135), NOT the per-position `state_dependent`. This corollary reinforces divergent: V3's per-position `state_dependent` is a genuinely new form with no V2 equivalent.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: State-dependent alpha stationary form alpha* = c0/(b0+D) and its regularizer
verifiedHighPoints.v3: alpha_i.py:82-84 alpha = c0/(b0+kl).clamp(min=eps); alpha_i.py:49-57 alpha_regularizer R = b0*alpha - c0*log(alpha)
verifiedHighPoints.v2: e_step.py:660 `return c0/(b0 + div_k)`; e_step.py:51-52 docstring R(alpha_i)=b0*alpha_i - c0*log alpha_i; the same Gamma-Normal conjugacy schedule
verifiedHighPoints.implication: The adaptive precision schedule and its log-barrier regularizer are mathematically identical on both sides; V3 just factors them into a registered form + an explicit alpha_regularizer helper. The envelope cancellation (alpha_i.py:138-163 alpha_gradient_coefficient) makes V2's product-rule correction a structural identity rather than a hand-maintained copy.

verdict.topic: State-dependent alpha stationary form alpha* = c0/(b0+D) and its regularizer
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/alpha_i.py:83-84 `alpha = c0 / (b0 + kl).clamp(min=eps)` ... `return alpha, alpha_regularizer(alpha, b0=b0, c0=c0)`; alpha_i.py:57 `return b0 * alpha - c0 * torch.log(alpha.clamp(min=1e-12))`; free_energy.py:290-291 `if alpha_reg is not None: self_term = self_term + alpha_reg` (R is ADDED into F, so the belief gradient envelope-cancels to alpha*·dD)
verdict.evidence_v2: e_step.py:660 `return c0 / (b0 + div_k)` (NO denominator clamp); e_step.py:798 aux self-term `self_term = (alpha_attached * div_qp_per_dim).sum() / token_count` is alpha*D with NO +R term; core/vfe_gradients.py:81 `grad_mu_self = grad_mu_self - (alpha ** 2 / alpha_c0) * kl_k * delta_mu / sigma_p_safe` — V2 HAND-ADDS the product-rule term alpha'(D)*D*dD to the belief gradient (descending un-regularized alpha*D); R(alpha)=b0*alpha-c0*log alpha appears ONLY in the docstring e_step.py:52, never computed/added.
verdict.finding: The closed-form alpha schedule alpha* = c0/(b0+D) is genuinely shared (V3 alpha_i.py:83 vs V2 e_step.py:660), differing only cosmetically: V3 clamps the denominator (b0+kl) at min=1e-12 (immaterial since b0,kl>=0), V2 does not. But the claim's "same" rests on the R(alpha) regularizer and the envelope-cancellation being shared, and there the two REPOS DIVERGE. V3 explicitly constructs R = b0*alpha - c0*log(alpha) (alpha_i.py:57) and ADDS it into F (free_energy.py:291). V2 never adds R to F: the actual F realized in V2 is the un-regularized alpha*D, both in the hyperparameter aux loss (e_step.py:798, `(alpha_attached*div).sum()`, no +R) and in the belief gradient, where V2 instead HAND-APPLIES the product-rule correction -(alpha^2/c0)*kl*dkl (vfe_gradients.py:81), i.e. the alpha'(D)*D*dD term that is the gradient of alpha*D. Consequence: under the state-dependent schedule (the regime this claim is about), the BELIEF-UPDATE GRADIENTS DIFFER. V3 descends alpha*·dD (R cancels the product-rule term via the envelope); V2 descends alpha*·dD - (alpha^2/c0)*D*dD (it keeps the product-rule term, because its F has no R to cancel it). These differ by exactly (alpha^2/c0)*D*dD, nonzero whenever D>0. The hyperparameter (c0,b0) gradients also differ: V2's dF/dc0 = D/(b0+D) (alpha*D objective, docstring e_step.py:726), whereas V3's regularized profile-F gives dF/dc0 = -log(alpha*) = log((b0+D)/c0). The claim's implication ("V3 just factors V2 into a registered form + the envelope makes V2's product-rule a structural identity") INVERTS the actual relationship: V2 keeps the product-rule term by descending un-regularized alpha*D, while V3 adds R and cancels it. They reach the SAME belief gradient only in the constant-alpha default; under the state-dependent precision schedule the claim names, the F functionals and resulting belief and hyperparameter gradients are not identical. Relationship is divergent, not same. (Advisor was rate-limited; conclusion rests on the literal kernel and free_energy code quoted above.)

dimensions.summary: V3 splits two concerns that V2 keeps fused-or-distributed: (1) a CENTRALIZED numerical-conditioning module (vfe3/numerics.py) with non-raising escalating-jitter Cholesky, SPD inverse with pinv fallback + ok-mask, eigenvalue floor, condition number, and a monitor registry — whereas V2's _numerics.py is a narrow E-step helper file (finiteness, trust-region, Frobenius clamp, diag KL/Renyi) and its actual SPD/Cholesky stabilization is scattered inline in core/vfe_gradients.py and prior_bank.py using raw torch.linalg.cholesky inside try/except+slogdet; (2) an explicit analytic-kernel-vs-autograd-ORACLE pair (gradients/kernels.py + gradients/oracle.py) where the hand kernel auto-falls-back to and is pinned against an autograd-of-F oracle computing the SAME filtering object. V2 also has both an analytic kernel (compute_vfe_gradients_gpu, in core/, out of the compared file) and an autograd path (_compute_mu_sigma_grad_autograd in e_step.py), but the V2 autograd path is a production opt-in computing a DIFFERENT (global key-side/smoothing F_red) object, FD-verified only in tests/ rather than serving as a runtime correctness fallback. Both repos FD-check gradients against autograd; the difference is placement (V3 production module vs V2 test-only) and which object is verified (filtering-matched vs global-key-side).

points.topic: Scope/role of the numerics module: centralized conditioning toolkit vs narrow E-step helper
points.v3: vfe3/numerics.py:1-191 — module is a CONDITIONING-FALLBACK + RUNTIME-MONITOR toolkit: safe_cholesky, safe_spd_inverse, floor_eigenvalues, condition_number, nan_inf_fraction, check_finite, plus a register_monitor registry (numerics.py:143-190)
points.v2: _numerics.py:1-308 — module is an E-step helper: check_finite (dict+abort modes), apply_mu_trust_region, cap_l2_norm, pre_exp_frobenius_clamp, diag_kl, diag_renyi. NO SPD inverse, eigenvalue floor, condition number, Cholesky helper, or monitor registry
points.implication: The two files only overlap on the NAME check_finite and the diagonal-KL formula. They are not the same module re-implemented; V3 centralizes SPD-stability primitives that V2 distributes inline, while V2's trust-region/Frobenius-clamp/diag_renyi helpers live elsewhere in V3 (outside the three compared files). Comparing the modules 1:1 is a category mismatch — they are differently-purposed files.

points.topic: Safe Cholesky: non-raising cholesky_ex + escalating per-element jitter + ok-mask vs raw cholesky + try/except + slogdet
points.v3: safe_cholesky (numerics.py:25-60) uses torch.linalg.cholesky_ex (never raises; per-element info), round-0 adds ZERO jitter (byte-identical to pure cholesky), retries ONLY failed elements with ridge eps*10^t, returns (L, ok-mask) so the caller injects NaN -> kl_max for failed elements
points.v2: n/a - absent in _numerics.py. V2's Cholesky stabilization is inline in core/vfe_gradients.py:614-619,629-634: raw torch.linalg.cholesky in try/except RuntimeError with a slogdet (logdet-only) fallback, plus eps*I (or gauge-covariant) ridge added before factoring
points.implication: Genuinely different failure semantics. V3 isolates a single non-PD batch element (cholesky_ex) and escalates jitter per-element while keeping good elements at zero-jitter, then routes failures to kl_max via an explicit ok-mask. V2's raw cholesky raises on ANY non-PD element in the batch, caught wholesale, and the fallback only recovers logdet (not a usable factor/inverse) — a coarser, value-biasing recovery. This affects numerical robustness and which inputs survive ill-conditioning.

points.topic: SPD inverse with escalating jitter and pinv fallback
points.v3: safe_spd_inverse (numerics.py:63-86): cholesky_inverse on M+eps*10^t I for t=0..max_tries-1, else torch.linalg.pinv; symmetrizes and casts to float first
points.v2: n/a - absent. V2 inverts SPD matrices via raw torch.cholesky_inverse after raw torch.linalg.cholesky (e.g. prior_bank.py:736-740) with no escalating-jitter or pinv fallback wrapper
points.implication: V3 provides a guaranteed-finite SPD inverse (pinv as last resort) as a reusable primitive; V2 has no equivalent guard around its SPD inversions, so a non-PD covariance raises mid-forward in V2 rather than degrading to a pseudo-inverse.

points.topic: Eigenvalue floor / SPD projection primitive
points.v3: floor_eigenvalues (numerics.py:89-99): eigh, clamp evals up to floor, reconstruct and re-symmetrize
points.v2: n/a - absent from _numerics.py. V2 floors eigenvalues inline in the prior/stack paths (prior_bank.py:731 _evals.clamp(min=1e-4,max=sigma_max); stack.py:159 eigvals.clamp(min=1e-4,max=sigma_max)) — clamps BOTH ends with a sigma_max ceiling
points.implication: Behavioral difference in clamp policy: V3's floor_eigenvalues applies a FLOOR only (no ceiling); V2's inline eigenvalue clamps apply [1e-4, sigma_max] with an upper bound to keep lambda_max finite. The V3 numerics primitive does not encode the sigma_max ceiling at all — that ceiling lives in V3's prior/decode layers (outside these files), so a golden-test parity check must confirm V3 reproduces V2's two-sided clamp where it matters.

points.topic: Condition-number monitor and runtime numerical-health registry
points.v3: condition_number (numerics.py:102-109) eigvalsh ratio lambda_max/lambda_min.clamp(eps); register_monitor/run_monitors registry (numerics.py:143-190) with nan_fraction, abs_max, condition_number probes
points.v2: n/a - absent. V2 tracks numerical health via ad-hoc trainer counters (trainer.py:1645 num_chol_recover/num_chol_fail/num_nan_replace/num_inv_pinv) incremented inline by _nan_guard, not a pluggable monitor registry
points.implication: Cosmetic/infra difference in how numerical health is surfaced: V3 a config-selectable monitor registry, V2 hard-coded named counters. No effect on the math; relevant only to diagnostics modularity.

points.topic: Explicit autograd-of-F oracle as a PRODUCTION module + runtime fallback
points.v3: gradients/oracle.py:50-118 belief_gradients_autograd is a production module: @torch.enable_grad island, differentiates free_energy w.r.t. (mu,sigma), and is the RUNTIME fallback for every non-kernel case (smoothing / gaussian_full / alpha_div!=1 / surrogate) per kernels.py:187-194
points.v2: _compute_mu_sigma_grad_autograd (e_step.py:2448-2534) is a production OPT-IN path (use_autograd_mu_sigma) computing the GLOBAL key-side F_red, plus _compute_rope_full_gauge_gradient_per_head (vfe_gradients.py:2030) autograd-per-head; autograd is a selectable production path, not a generic correctness fallback behind the analytic kernel
points.implication: Core structural divergence in gradient infrastructure. V3 treats autograd-of-F as the always-available source of truth that the hand kernel falls back to and is pinned against (same filtering object). V2's autograd paths are alternative production computations (global/key-side, or rope-full-gauge), each computing a DIFFERENT object than its analytic kernel — there is no single autograd oracle the V2 analytic kernel defers to for unsupported families.

points.topic: What the analytic kernel vs the oracle/autograd computes (filtering query-side object)
points.v3: kernels.py:80-139 _diag_kl_filtering_kernel computes the query-side (row) gradient with self-saturation mask; pinned to the FILTERING oracle (oracle.py gradient_mode='filtering', keys detached at oracle.py:95)
points.v2: compute_vfe_gradients_gpu (core/vfe_gradients.py:1791) / _fused_attention_and_vfe_gradients_block_diag (:1133) compute the query-side filtering gradient (sum_i F_i, other q_j fixed), per the e_step.py:2485-2494 docstring contrast
points.implication: The DEFAULT analytic object matches across repos: both compute the query-side/filtering (mean-field, keys-fixed) belief gradient as the production kernel. This is the equivalence the V3 golden tests must preserve; it is the load-bearing 'same' that justifies the clean-room claim. V3's oracle verifies exactly this object; V2 verifies it only indirectly (its FD test targets the global path instead).

points.topic: Finite-difference gradient check: production-module-pinned vs test-only
points.v3: Dedicated test files tests/test_gradients_oracle.py and tests/test_gradients_kernels.py pin the kernel to the oracle; the oracle itself ships in the package (gradients/oracle.py) and is reused at runtime
points.v2: tests/test_keyside_gradient_objective.py:114-122 does a central-difference FD check (eps=1e-6, (Fp-Fm)/(2*eps)) of F_total vs autograd, and :146-211 checks the production autograd path matches a reference autograd.grad of F_red — FD verification exists, but only in tests, and it targets the GLOBAL key-side object
points.implication: Both repos FD-check gradients against autograd, so 'V2 has no gradient verification' would be false. The genuine difference is placement and target: V3 elevates the autograd-of-F to a reusable production oracle pinned against the FILTERING kernel; V2 keeps FD verification test-only and aimed at the GLOBAL/key-side path. Equivalent rigor, different architecture.

points.topic: create_graph / unrolled-E-step differentiability handling
points.v3: oracle.py:87-118 use_live = create_graph and mu/sigma.requires_grad keeps a live leaf so the unrolled-through-inference grad reaches the prior; else detached clone + .detach() result
points.v2: vfe_gradients.py:2329-2336 _compute_rope_full_gauge_gradient_per_head passes create_graph=_need_phi_graph/retain_graph so dF/dmu,dF/dsigma stay differentiable w.r.t. phi for the unrolled phi step
points.implication: Both repos carry a create_graph mechanism to keep the autograd belief-gradient differentiable through the unrolled E-step (so signal reaches phi/prior). The intent matches; V3 generalizes it (live-leaf vs detached-clone branch) across all families rather than only the rope-full-gauge per-head path.

points.topic: Diagonal KL formula (raw value)
points.v3: _raw_diag_kl / _raw_diag_kl_per_coord (kernels.py:37-77): 0.5*(sq/sp + (mu_p-mu_q)^2/sp - K + log(sp)-log(sq)), eps=1e-6 floor
points.v2: diag_kl (_numerics.py:224-256): 0.5*(sq/sp + (mu_q-mu_p)^2/sp - 1 + log(sp)-log(sq)) per-dim, eps=1e-8 floor
points.implication: The KL expression is identical (the (mu_q-mu_p)^2 squared term is sign-symmetric). The eps floor differs (V3 1e-6 vs V2 1e-8), but V3's _raw_diag_kl value feeds the SATURATION MASK, not the KL that must numerically match V2 (that flows through V3's divergence/safe_kl_clamp seam, outside these files). So this is not a numerical divergence in the matched object; the discriminating golden-test check is whether V3's DIVERGENCE-SEAM KL (not the mask path) shares V2's 1e-8 floor.

points.topic: Diagonal Renyi-alpha divergence helper
points.v3: n/a - absent from the three compared files. V3's kernel only handles alpha_div==1 (KL) analytically (kernels.py:182-183 abs(alpha_div-1)<1e-9) and routes alpha!=1 to the autograd oracle; the closed-form diagonal Renyi lives in V3's family/divergence layer, not numerics.py or kernels.py
points.v2: diag_renyi (_numerics.py:259-296): closed-form per-dim Gaussian Renyi-R_alpha with blended variance sigma_blend=(1-alpha)sq+alpha*sp, singular at alpha=1
points.implication: Within the compared file sets, V2 ships a closed-form diagonal Renyi primitive in _numerics.py while V3 has no analytic Renyi in numerics.py/kernels.py (it falls back to autograd for alpha!=1). Relative to the compared files this is v2_only; it is NOT an absolute absence in V3 (the Renyi divergence exists in V3's divergence_family='renyi' seam consumed by the oracle).

points.topic: check_finite: same name, divergent API and NaN-response strategy
points.v3: numerics.py:121-137 check_finite(tensor, name, raise_on_nonfinite=False) -> bool, built on nan_inf_fraction; warns or raises FloatingPointError; companion strategy is a guard/monitor, not in-place repair
points.v2: _numerics.py:49-98 check_finite(tensors:Dict, mode in off/warn/revert/abort, step_label) -> bool, raises VFENonFiniteError carrying step_label+field; the in-place NaN-REPAIR strategy is the separate _nan_guard (core/vfe_gradients.py:149-162, torch.where replace-with-fill + counter)
points.implication: The shared name hides a real API/behavior divergence: V2's check_finite is a multi-tensor abort/revert sentinel tied to E-step iterate snapshots, and V2 additionally REPAIRS NaNs in-place via _nan_guard (replace-with-fill, biasing the value but continuing). V3's check_finite is a single-tensor finiteness probe; V3 instead routes Cholesky failures to kl_max via the safe_cholesky ok-mask rather than replace-with-fill. Different NaN philosophies (mask-and-saturate vs replace-and-count) that can produce different finite outputs on degenerate inputs.

points.topic: Trust-region / pre-exp Frobenius clamp / L2 cap primitives
points.v2: apply_mu_trust_region (_numerics.py:101-162, box+ball modes), cap_l2_norm (:165-172), pre_exp_frobenius_clamp (:175-221, bounds ||X||_F before matrix_exp to cap the sandwich condition number)
points.v3: n/a - absent from the three compared files (numerics.py/kernels.py/oracle.py). Grep over vfe3/ for these symbol names returns no matches, so these specific helpers are not present under these names anywhere in V3's package
points.implication: V2's E-step step-size/condition-control primitives (sigma-whitened delta_mu clamp, natural-grad L2 cap, pre-matrix_exp Frobenius clamp) have no same-named counterpart in V3 at all (confirmed by grep). If V3 relies on these guards, they are implemented under different names in the retraction/geometry layer; the pre_exp_frobenius_clamp condition-number control in particular is a numerical-stability mechanism a parity audit should confirm V3 reproduces somewhere.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Safe Cholesky: non-raising cholesky_ex + escalating per-element jitter + ok-mask vs raw cholesky + try/except + slogdet
verifiedHighPoints.v3: safe_cholesky (numerics.py:25-60) uses torch.linalg.cholesky_ex (never raises; per-element info), round-0 adds ZERO jitter (byte-identical to pure cholesky), retries ONLY failed elements with ridge eps*10^t, returns (L, ok-mask) so the caller injects NaN -> kl_max for failed elements
verifiedHighPoints.v2: n/a - absent in _numerics.py. V2's Cholesky stabilization is inline in core/vfe_gradients.py:614-619,629-634: raw torch.linalg.cholesky in try/except RuntimeError with a slogdet (logdet-only) fallback, plus eps*I (or gauge-covariant) ridge added before factoring
verifiedHighPoints.implication: Genuinely different failure semantics. V3 isolates a single non-PD batch element (cholesky_ex) and escalates jitter per-element while keeping good elements at zero-jitter, then routes failures to kl_max via an explicit ok-mask. V2's raw cholesky raises on ANY non-PD element in the batch, caught wholesale, and the fallback only recovers logdet (not a usable factor/inverse) — a coarser, value-biasing recovery. This affects numerical robustness and which inputs survive ill-conditioning.

verdict.topic: Safe Cholesky: non-raising cholesky_ex + escalating per-element jitter + ok-mask vs raw cholesky + try/except + slogdet
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/numerics.py:48-60 safe_cholesky: "L, info = torch.linalg.cholesky_ex(M)" / "ok = info == 0" / "L_t, info_t = torch.linalg.cholesky_ex(M + (eps * (10.0 ** t)) * eye)" / "newly = (~ok) & (info_t == 0)" / "L = torch.where(newly...L_t, L)" / "return L, ok"
verdict.evidence_v2: core/vfe_utils.py:242-270 _safe_spd_inv (called live at core/vfe_gradients.py:643 "_safe_spd_inv(sigma_i_block_diag, eps=TRANSPORT_JITTER)"): "L, info = torch.linalg.cholesky_ex(M_reg)" / "failed = (info != 0)" / "current_reg = current_reg * 10.0" / "L_retry, info_retry = torch.linalg.cholesky_ex(M_reg_more)" / "newly_fixed = failed & (info_retry == 0)" / "result = torch.where(mask_expand, inv_retry, result)". Factor-returning analogue: core/kl_computation.py:242-254 _cholesky_with_fallback "L, info = torch.linalg.cholesky_ex(mat)" + 5-round escalation returning L. Cited raw-cholesky+slogdet path is real at core/vfe_gradients.py:614-619 "L_j = torch.linalg.cholesky(sigma_j_reg)" / "except RuntimeError:" / "sign_j, logdet_j = torch.linalg.slogdet(sigma_j_reg)".
verdict.finding: The claim's V2 description is partly accurate but its load-bearing assertions are false. ACCURATE: core/vfe_gradients.py:613-634 does use raw torch.linalg.cholesky in try/except RuntimeError with a slogdet logdet-only fallback plus an eps*I (or gauge-covariant exp_phi@exp_phi^T) ridge — exactly as cited. FALSE (refutes "divergent"): the claim says the cholesky_ex + per-element info + escalating-per-element-jitter + ok-mask technique is "absent" in V2 and that V2's fallback "only recovers logdet (not a usable factor/inverse)." V2 has that exact technique live in core/vfe_utils.py:_safe_spd_inv (cholesky_ex line 242; failed=(info!=0) line 253; 10x escalation applied ONLY to failed elements via torch.where line 270), and it recovers a USABLE INVERSE — directly contradicting the "logdet-only" recovery claim. This function is production-reachable, called at vfe_gradients.py:643. A factor-returning analogue (returns L, not an inverse) also exists at core/kl_computation.py:_cholesky_with_fallback (cholesky_ex + info + 5-round jitter escalation), though its own audit comment flags the escalation branch as test-only/DENSE-path. So V3's safe_cholesky is not a genuinely new failure-semantics primitive; it is a CONSOLIDATION/REFACTOR of three scattered V2 sites (_safe_spd_inv, _cholesky_with_fallback, the inline vfe_gradients raw-cholesky path) into one reusable (L, ok-mask) primitive. Real refinements exist (round-0 zero extra jitter so the factor is byte-identical to pure cholesky on SPD inputs; returning an ok-mask so the caller injects NaN->kl_max instead of V2's raise->NaN-KL or pinv last resort), but these are interface/refactoring-level differences with converging end semantics (failed element -> NaN KL -> kl_max), not divergent algorithms. Caveat: the claim's stated V2 root is transformer/vfe/, but the cited and analogue files all live in transformer/core/ (a sibling package); the flat transformer/vfe/_numerics.py indeed contains no safe_cholesky, which is the narrow sense in which the "absent in _numerics.py" remark is literally true yet misleading about V2 as a whole.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Explicit autograd-of-F oracle as a PRODUCTION module + runtime fallback
verifiedHighPoints.v3: gradients/oracle.py:50-118 belief_gradients_autograd is a production module: @torch.enable_grad island, differentiates free_energy w.r.t. (mu,sigma), and is the RUNTIME fallback for every non-kernel case (smoothing / gaussian_full / alpha_div!=1 / surrogate) per kernels.py:187-194
verifiedHighPoints.v2: _compute_mu_sigma_grad_autograd (e_step.py:2448-2534) is a production OPT-IN path (use_autograd_mu_sigma) computing the GLOBAL key-side F_red, plus _compute_rope_full_gauge_gradient_per_head (vfe_gradients.py:2030) autograd-per-head; autograd is a selectable production path, not a generic correctness fallback behind the analytic kernel
verifiedHighPoints.implication: Core structural divergence in gradient infrastructure. V3 treats autograd-of-F as the always-available source of truth that the hand kernel falls back to and is pinned against (same filtering object). V2's autograd paths are alternative production computations (global/key-side, or rope-full-gauge), each computing a DIFFERENT object than its analytic kernel — there is no single autograd oracle the V2 analytic kernel defers to for unsupported families.

verdict.topic: Explicit autograd-of-F oracle as a PRODUCTION module + runtime fallback
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/gradients/kernels.py:187-194 "if not use_kernel: return belief_gradients_autograd(...)" — the oracle is the runtime fallback for every non-kernel case (smoothing/gaussian_full/alpha_div!=1/no-entropy); kernels.py:1-15 oracle is "the correctness source of truth" and "the hand kernels are pinned to the FILTERING oracle"; tests/test_gradients_kernels.py:19-22 test_kernel_matches_filtering_oracle_constant_alpha asserts belief_gradients == belief_gradients_autograd(gradient_mode="filtering") — SAME object
verdict.evidence_v2: e_step.py:1367-1380 "if self.use_autograd_mu_sigma: grad_mu,grad_sigma = self._compute_mu_sigma_grad_autograd(...) else: ... compute_vfe_gradients_gpu(... alpha_div=self.alpha_divergence ... irrep_dims=self.irrep_dims ...)"; e_step.py:2485-2494 docstring "The default analytic kernel compute_vfe_gradients_gpu ... computes a different object: the query-side partial only"; e_step.py:2535-2543 autograd path RAISES RuntimeError when alpha_divergence!=1 (opposite of a generic fallback); vfe_gradients.py:2030 _compute_rope_full_gauge_gradient_per_head is a separate opt-in (use_rope AND rope_full_gauge!='off') autograd-per-head path
verdict.finding: The divergence holds and is, if anything, understated. V3's belief_gradients_autograd is the single always-available oracle: it computes the SAME object (filtering) the hand kernel does (pinned byte-equal by test_kernel_matches_filtering_oracle_constant_alpha), and the dispatcher unconditionally falls back to it for every family the kernel cannot serve (smoothing, gaussian_full, alpha_div!=1, no-entropy) via "if not use_kernel: return belief_gradients_autograd(...)". V2 inverts this on every axis: (1) autograd is gated strictly behind the config toggle use_autograd_mu_sigma, not a correctness fallback — the analytic kernels (compute_vfe_gradients_gpu, _fused_attention_and_vfe_gradients_block_diag) are the workhorse and themselves handle alpha_div, irrep_dims, per-head, and full-cov, so there is no "kernel can't do this family, defer to autograd" path; (2) the autograd path computes a DIFFERENT object than the analytic kernel — the GLOBAL key-side F_red (self + query + key/column) vs the kernel's query-side filtering partial (docstring 2485-2494), so it is not a pin/oracle for the kernel; (3) far from being a generic correctness fallback, the V2 autograd path REFUSES alpha_div!=1 with a RuntimeError (2535-2543) — exactly the family V3 routes TO its oracle; (4) the fused analytic path is explicitly disabled when the autograd toggle is on (e_step.py:1202 "and not self.use_autograd_mu_sigma"), confirming they are mutually-exclusive alternatives, not kernel+fallback. The second V2 autograd entry point, _compute_rope_full_gauge_gradient_per_head (vfe_gradients.py:2030), is likewise a separate opt-in (use_rope AND rope_full_gauge!='off') computing the R-Sigma-R^T sandwich object — another alternative production computation, not a shared oracle. No misread on either side; the comparing agent's structural characterization is accurate.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: What the analytic kernel vs the oracle/autograd computes (filtering query-side object)
verifiedHighPoints.v3: kernels.py:80-139 _diag_kl_filtering_kernel computes the query-side (row) gradient with self-saturation mask; pinned to the FILTERING oracle (oracle.py gradient_mode='filtering', keys detached at oracle.py:95)
verifiedHighPoints.v2: compute_vfe_gradients_gpu (core/vfe_gradients.py:1791) / _fused_attention_and_vfe_gradients_block_diag (:1133) compute the query-side filtering gradient (sum_i F_i, other q_j fixed), per the e_step.py:2485-2494 docstring contrast
verifiedHighPoints.implication: The DEFAULT analytic object matches across repos: both compute the query-side/filtering (mean-field, keys-fixed) belief gradient as the production kernel. This is the equivalence the V3 golden tests must preserve; it is the load-bearing 'same' that justifies the clean-room claim. V3's oracle verifies exactly this object; V2 verifies it only indirectly (its FD test targets the global path instead).

verdict.topic: What the analytic kernel vs the oracle/autograd computes (filtering query-side object)
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/gradients/kernels.py:127 "self_mask = ((raw_self > 0.0) & (raw_self < kl_max))..."; kernels.py:132 "self_mu = self_mask * alpha_coef * (mu_q - mu_p) / sp"; kernels.py:115 "The pairwise term needs no mask"; default kl_max=100.0 (kernels.py:92), flat clamp via vfe3/families/base.py:26 "kl = kl.clamp(min=0.0, max=kl_max)"
verdict.evidence_v2: core/vfe_gradients.py:848 "grad_mu_self = alpha * delta_mu / sigma_p_safe" and :1259 (fused, LIVE path) "grad_mu_self = alpha * delta_mu_sp / sigma_p_safe" — NO self-saturation mask; the only mask is on the PAIR term and only under surrogate, :1077 "if lambda_softmax > 0:" / :1078 "_unsat = (kl_for_coupling < max(KL_CEIL_BASE, KL_CEIL_SCALE * K))"; ceiling is K-dependent via vfe_utils.py:63-64 "KL_CEIL_BASE: float = 100.0 / KL_CEIL_SCALE: float = 5.0"
verdict.finding: The high-level claim (both repos' default analytic kernel computes the query-side/filtering gradient of sum_i F_i with keys held fixed) is correct in framing — confirmed by e_step.py:2485-2494 and oracle.py:94-95 (keys detached). The mu/sigma self+pair formulas share the same algebraic skeleton. But "same" (bit-identical pinned object) does NOT survive. Two concrete code-level divergences in the SELF-coupling term: (1) Saturation mask is INVERTED/RELOCATED. V3 multiplies the self term (both mu and sigma) by self_mask = 1[0 < D(q_i||p_i) < kl_max] and explicitly leaves the pair term unmasked (kernels.py:115,127,132,136). V2's diagonal AND fused-primary self-coupling (vfe_gradients.py:848-849, 1259-1260) carry NO self mask and never clamp the self-divergence at all; V2's ONLY saturation gate is on the pair/softmax-coupling term, and only when lambda_softmax>0 (the surrogate, :1077-1080). So at any token whose belief is far from its prior (raw self-divergence >= kl_max) or exactly at it (=0), V3 zeroes the self gradient while V2 keeps emitting alpha*delta_mu/sigma_p — the two kernels produce different numbers there. (2) The clamp ceiling differs: V3 default kl_max=100.0 flat (base.py:26); V2 uses max(100.0, 5.0*K) (vfe_utils.py:63-64), K-dependent, so for K>20 even the pairwise-energy saturation point differs. Each kernel is pinned to its OWN repo's oracle/objective, and those objectives are not the identical function — V3's sum_i F_i clamps and differentiates through a flat-100 self-divergence; V2's never clamps the self-divergence. Same object CLASS (query-side filtering gradient), divergent computed values at saturation plus a different clamp constant. Note V2's learnable-alpha product-rule correction (_alpha_product_rule_diag_kl) is a separate, additional self-term branch gated on alpha_c0 — not a saturation mask — and has no V3 counterpart in the kernel path, a further self-term divergence under that toggle.

dimensions.dimension: Data loading + metrics + visualization (tooling around the model)
dimensions.summary: V3's data/metrics/viz tooling is a leaner, registry-backed reimplementation that delegates the heavy parts V2 owned inline. DATA is the largest asymmetry: V2's datasets.py (3138 lines) is a full tokenization pipeline (tiktoken/byte tokenizers, downloads/caching, strides, vocab mapping, tokens_per_char) while V3 (155 lines) is a thin causal-window loader over a pre-tokenized cache it never builds. METRICS overlap in spirit (effective rank, attention entropy, holonomy, gauge invariants) but diverge in two consequential ways: V3 uses the participation-ratio effective rank vs V2's exponential-entropy effective rank, and V3's BPC is bits-per-token (ce/ln2) with no tokens_per_char char-correction that V2's training/bpc.py applies; V2's cross_coupling_metrics are super-block-partitioned with no V3 metrics.py counterpart. VIZ both ship UMAP belief figures over (mu, sigma, phi), but V3 colors a single Euclidean/log-Euclidean UMAP by linguistic token taxonomy with native-space silhouette, while V2's semantic_clustering builds geometry-faithful precomputed distance matrices (Bhattacharyya, log-Euclidean, GL+ Omega-geodesic) then projects+clusters+auto-k; V2 additionally ships cross_coupling_viz super-block diagnostics absent from V3.

points.topic: Dataset module scope: tokenization pipeline vs thin window loader
points.v3: vfe3/data/datasets.py:96-155 — TokenWindows + make_dataloader slice a pre-tokenized 1-D cache (.pt or .bin memmap, load_cached_tokens:46-73) into causal (input,target) windows; 155 lines total, no tokenizer, no download, no CLI
points.v2: transformer/data/datasets.py:1280 WikiText2TiktokenDataset (~850 lines) plus WikiText2Dataset:946, char/byte variants:2136/2354, _download_*:283/353, create_dataloaders:2711; 3138 lines owning tiktoken/byte tokenization, HTTP download, caching, strides, vocab restriction
points.implication: V3 delegates ALL tokenization/caching/vocab-mapping/byte-mode/stride machinery to an out-of-band pre-built cache; the model-facing contract (1-D token stream -> shifted windows) is preserved but every corpus-prep concern V2 owned is dropped. Reproducing a V2 run in V3 requires the cache to already exist with the matching tokenizer tag (_tokenizer_tag:28).

points.topic: BPC units: char-corrected bits-per-character vs bits-per-token
points.v3: vfe3/train.py:290 bpc = ce / math.log(2.0) — bits-per-token; no tokens_per_char factor exists anywhere in vfe3 (grep: 0 hits)
points.v2: transformer/training/bpc.py:69-79 compute_bpc = (ce/ln2) * tokens_per_char, ratio read from dataset (datasets.py:1498/1902 tokens_per_char = n_tokens/n_chars); warns loudly when absent
points.implication: V3's 'bpc' is bits-per-token mislabeled as BPC; it differs from V2's true bits-per-character by 1/tokens_per_char (~4x for gpt2-BPE English, ~10x-class for cl100k Japanese). Cross-repo BPC numbers are NOT comparable, and V3 cannot report honest BPC because the cache carries no char count.

points.topic: Cross-entropy / PPL core computation
points.v3: vfe3/train.py:279-289 token-weighted CE = sum(ce_b * n_b)/sum(n_b) over non-(-100) targets; ppl = exp(min(ce,20))
points.v2: transformer/vfe/trainer.py:1377/1491/1585 ppl = math.exp(min(ce_val,20.0)); same token-weighted unscaled CE as PPL/BPC source of truth
points.implication: The headline CE and PPL (the clamp at 20, token-weighting, ignore_index=-100) match V2 exactly, so PPL is directly comparable across repos. Only the BPC char-correction and the F-regularizer separation differ; the data term itself is pinned.

points.topic: effective_rank definition: participation ratio vs exponential entropy

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Dataset module scope: tokenization pipeline vs thin window loader
verifiedHighPoints.v3: vfe3/data/datasets.py:96-155 — TokenWindows + make_dataloader slice a pre-tokenized 1-D cache (.pt or .bin memmap, load_cached_tokens:46-73) into causal (input,target) windows; 155 lines total, no tokenizer, no download, no CLI
verifiedHighPoints.v2: transformer/data/datasets.py:1280 WikiText2TiktokenDataset (~850 lines) plus WikiText2Dataset:946, char/byte variants:2136/2354, _download_*:283/353, create_dataloaders:2711; 3138 lines owning tiktoken/byte tokenization, HTTP download, caching, strides, vocab restriction
verifiedHighPoints.implication: V3 delegates ALL tokenization/caching/vocab-mapping/byte-mode/stride machinery to an out-of-band pre-built cache; the model-facing contract (1-D token stream -> shifted windows) is preserved but every corpus-prep concern V2 owned is dropped. Reproducing a V2 run in V3 requires the cache to already exist with the matching tokenizer tag (_tokenizer_tag:28).

verdict.topic: Dataset module scope: tokenization pipeline vs thin window loader
verdict.evidence_v3: C:/Users/chris and christine/Desktop/V3_Transformer/vfe3/data/datasets.py:111 `self.stride = seq_len if stride is None else stride`; :121-123 `start = idx * self.stride` ... `return self.tokens[start:end], self.tokens[start + 1:end + 1]`; :115 `self.n = usable // self.stride + 1`. Caller train_vfe3.py:252 `make_dataloader(dataset, split, cfg.max_seq_len, cfg.batch_size, shuffle=is_train, drop_last=is_train, max_tokens=cap)` passes NO stride (relies on default).
verdict.evidence_v2: C:/Users/chris and christine/Desktop/VFE_2.0/transformer/data/datasets.py (WikiText2TiktokenDataset, class 1280-2135): :1910-1912 `if self.stride is None: # LEGACY PATH ... self.num_sequences = max(1, len(self.tokens) - self.max_seq_len)`; :2106-2108 `if self.stride is None: ... start_idx = idx`; :2117-2118 `input_ids = self.tokens[start_idx:end_idx].long(); target_ids = self.tokens[start_idx + 1:end_idx + 1].long()`. create_dataloaders:2722 `stride: Optional[int] = None`.
verdict.finding: The same sentinel default (stride=None) means OPPOSITE things in the two modules, a factor-of-seq_len difference in window density that the equivalence gloss hides. V2 stride=None routes to the LEGACY PATH: start_idx=idx (dense, stride-1) with num_sequences = N - T, so ~N overlapping windows. V3 stride=None sets self.stride = seq_len (datasets.py:111), giving start = idx*seq_len (non-overlapping) and n = usable//seq_len + 1, so ~N/seq_len windows. The per-window slicing is byte-identical in both (input=tokens[i:i+L], target=tokens[i+1:i+L+1]), so this is a slimmed re-implementation of V2's windowing with a changed default, not a change of purpose -- the label "refactored" survives (holds=true; "divergent" would overstate it). This is a LIVE divergence, not a dormant module-only trap: the V3 production caller train_vfe3.py:252 passes no stride, so real V3 runs see ~seq_len-times fewer, non-overlapping windows than a default V2 run over the same token stream. What is REFUTED is the claim's implication, not the relationship label: "the model-facing contract (1-D stream -> shifted windows) is preserved" is incomplete because window count/density is part of that contract and differs at the default; and "reproducing a V2 run in V3 requires the cache to already exist" understates it -- reproduction also requires aligning the stride (V2 stride=max_seq_len, or V3 explicit stride=1). Secondary, minor: V2 pads partial tail windows (pad_token_id / -100 ignore_index, :2120-2131) while V3 has no padding and drops the tail via drop_last=True; this further perturbs which tokens are scored but is small next to the stride-default difference.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: BPC units: char-corrected bits-per-character vs bits-per-token
verifiedHighPoints.v3: vfe3/train.py:290 bpc = ce / math.log(2.0) — bits-per-token; no tokens_per_char factor exists anywhere in vfe3 (grep: 0 hits)
verifiedHighPoints.v2: transformer/training/bpc.py:69-79 compute_bpc = (ce/ln2) * tokens_per_char, ratio read from dataset (datasets.py:1498/1902 tokens_per_char = n_tokens/n_chars); warns loudly when absent
verifiedHighPoints.implication: V3's 'bpc' is bits-per-token mislabeled as BPC; it differs from V2's true bits-per-character by 1/tokens_per_char (~4x for gpt2-BPE English, ~10x-class for cl100k Japanese). Cross-repo BPC numbers are NOT comparable, and V3 cannot report honest BPC because the cache carries no char count.

verdict.topic: BPC units: char-corrected bits-per-character vs bits-per-token
verdict.evidence_v3: C:/Users/chris and christine/Desktop/V3_Transformer/vfe3/train.py:290 `"bpc": ce / math.log(2.0),` (docstring train.py:261 `\mathrm{BPC} = \mathrm{CE} / \ln 2`). Grep over the ENTIRE V3 repo (*.py) for tokens_per_char|n_chars|bits.per.char|per_char = 0 hits; vfe3/data/datasets.py grep for char|byte|tokens_per = 0 hits.
verdict.evidence_v2: C:/Users/chris and christine/Desktop/VFE_2.0/transformer/training/bpc.py:69 `bits_per_token = float(ce_nats) / math.log(2.0)` then :79 `return bits_per_token * float(tokens_per_char)`, with loud fallback warning :71-78. Ratio sourced at transformer/data/datasets.py:1498 and :1902 `'tokens_per_char': float(n_tokens_total)/max(1,int(self._n_chars_source))` / `self._tokens_per_char = float(len(self.tokens))/float(self._n_chars_source)`.
verdict.finding: Claim survives the adversarial attack — it is genuinely divergent on every substantive point. V3's "bpc" (train.py:290, and its own docstring at :261) is exactly CE/ln2 = bits-per-token, with the bits-per-token value propagated repo-wide under the BPC label (run_artifacts.py:201-216 test_bpc, viz/figures.py val-BPC axes and delta-BPC ablation forests). No tokens_per_char / n_chars / per_char symbol exists anywhere in the V3 repo, and vfe3/data/datasets.py caches no char or byte count, so V3 genuinely cannot derive an honest BPC. V2 explicitly multiplies by the BPE compression ratio (bpc.py:79) read from the dataset (datasets.py:1498/1902 = n_tokens/n_chars) and warns loudly when it must fall back to bits-per-token (bpc.py:71-78). The two numbers differ by the multiplicative factor tokens_per_char, so cross-repo BPC values are not comparable. The ONLY error in the comparing agent's writeup is a benign path-prefix slip: it wrote the V2 file as transformer/vfe/transformer/training/bpc.py, but since the supplied V2 root already ends in transformer/vfe the real path is transformer/training/bpc.py (i.e. .../VFE_2.0/transformer/training/bpc.py). The content at every cited line number (bpc.py:69-79, datasets.py:1498, :1902) matches verbatim, so this is a doubled-directory typo in the path, not a misread of the code. corrected_relationship is left as divergent.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Cross-entropy / PPL core computation
verifiedHighPoints.v3: vfe3/train.py:279-289 token-weighted CE = sum(ce_b * n_b)/sum(n_b) over non-(-100) targets; ppl = exp(min(ce,20))
verifiedHighPoints.v2: transformer/vfe/trainer.py:1377/1491/1585 ppl = math.exp(min(ce_val,20.0)); same token-weighted unscaled CE as PPL/BPC source of truth
verifiedHighPoints.implication: The headline CE and PPL (the clamp at 20, token-weighting, ignore_index=-100) match V2 exactly, so PPL is directly comparable across repos. Only the BPC char-correction and the F-regularizer separation differ; the data term itself is pinned.

verdict.topic: Cross-entropy / PPL core computation
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/model/model.py:407 `ce = F.cross_entropy(flat_logits, flat_targets, ignore_index=-100)`; :413 `loss = ce`; :539 `return logits, loss, ce.detach()` (regularizers added to `loss` only, so the reported CE is unscaled/regularizer-free). vfe3/train.py:279-283 `total_nats += float(ce) * n_b` / `total_tok += n_b` / `ce = total_nats / max(total_tok, 1)` with :278 `n_b = int((targets != -100).sum())`; :289 `"ppl": math.exp(min(ce, 20.0))`.
verdict.evidence_v2: transformer/vfe/model.py:298-302 `ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, self.cfg.vocab_size), targets.view(-1), ignore_index=-100)`; :308 `ce_for_log = ce_loss.detach()` (taken BEFORE the optional 1/sqrt(K) scaling at :316-318). trainer.py:1377 `ppl = math.exp(min(ce_val, 20.0))`; :1467 `ce_contrib = ce_for_log * n_tokens_t.to(ce_for_log.dtype)` with :1466 `n_tokens_t = (target_ids != -100).sum()`; :1478/:1584 `avg_ce = total_ce / max(total_tokens, 1)`; :1491/:1585 `math.exp(min(avg_ce, 20.0))`.
verdict.finding: The CE/PPL core computation is genuinely equivalent at the formula level: both repos compute the per-batch CE as the mean `F.cross_entropy(..., ignore_index=-100)` over flattened logits/targets, detach it before any regularizer or 1/sqrt(K) scaling enters the optimizer loss, aggregate token-weighted by the non-(-100) count, divide by max(total_tok,1), and clamp `exp(min(ce,20.0))`. I confirmed the load-bearing gap the equivalence rested on: V3's reported CE is `ce.detach()` (model.py:539), unscaled and regularizer-free, mirroring V2's `ce_for_log = ce_loss.detach()` taken pre-scaling (model.py:308). V3 has no `normalize_ce_by_dim` knob on the reported CE at all, so it is the raw mean either way. Three real-but-non-flipping differences exist, none touching the headline formula: (1) V3's all-ignore microbatch is guarded (model.py:412 `ce = flat_logits.sum() * 0.0`; prior_bank.py:369), whereas V2's model.py:298 path is unguarded and returns NaN on an all-`-100` batch — a robustness/degenerate-batch divergence, not a formula one; (2) V3 accumulates `total_nats` in Python float/double on the host while V2 accumulates `ce_contrib` in an fp32 GPU tensor — same token-weighted mean, differing only in float accumulation order/precision; (3) V3's `evaluate` caps via `max_batches` (process-then-break, train.py:281) while V2 caps via `max_samples`/windows checked at loop top — a coverage/data-loading semantics difference on a different dimension. Scope caution: the claim's broader implication that "PPL is directly comparable across repos" additionally requires the decode kernel feeding the logits to match (a separate topic not verified here); the formula/aggregation/clamp equivalence asserted by THIS topic holds.

verifiedHighPoints.v2: cross_coupling_metrics.py:309 eff_rank = exp(-Σ p log p) with p=λ/Σλ; same exp-entropy form in semantic_clustering/metrics.py:173 sigma_metrics
verifiedHighPoints.implication: Two genuinely different effective-rank functionals share the name. Both equal K for a flat spectrum and decrease under concentration, but the numbers differ for any non-flat spectrum (participation ratio falls faster). A V3-vs-V2 'effective rank' comparison is not apples-to-apples; this is a real numerical divergence, not a rename.

verdict.topic: effective_rank definition: participation ratio vs exponential entropy
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/metrics.py:36 "return (s1 * s1 / s2).to(spectrum.dtype)" where s1=lam.sum(-1), s2=(lam**2).sum(-1) — i.e. (Sigma lam)^2 / Sigma lam^2, the spectral participation ratio. Registered metric _m_eff_rank at metrics.py:850 calls effective_rank(sigma); used across-layer via effective_rank_per_token at metrics.py:185/268. Grep over all of vfe3 finds NO exp-entropy effective-rank variant.
verdict.evidence_v2: cross_coupling_metrics.py:308-309 "ent = -(p * p.clamp(min=eps).log()).sum(dim=-1)" then "eff_rank = ent.exp()" with p=s_h/s_h.sum; and semantic_clustering/metrics.py:171-173 "p = lam / lam.sum(...)", "entropy = -(p * np.log(...)).sum(-1)", "eff_rank = np.exp(entropy)". Grep over all of V2 (excl. pure_fep/coupled_fep) finds NO (Sigma lam)^2/Sigma lam^2 participation-ratio effective-rank form; every eff_rank site routes through one of these two exp-entropy definitions.
verdict.finding: Attempted refutation fails on every front. (1) Not a rename: the two functionals are mathematically distinct — V3 computes the Renyi-2-based participation ratio (Sigma lam)^2/Sigma lam^2, V2 computes the Shannon exponential entropy exp(-Sigma p log p), p=lam/Sigma lam. Both equal K for a flat spectrum and decrease under concentration, but for any non-flat spectrum participation ratio < exp-entropy (it weights large modes more heavily, falling faster), so the reported numbers differ. (2) The "missing" form does not exist on the other side under another name: grep confirms V3 has no exp-entropy effective rank and V2 has no participation-ratio effective rank (every V2 effective_rank site at cross_coupling_metrics.py:309, semantic_clustering/metrics.py:173, and their callers trainer.py:1273 / run_semantic_clustering.py:175 uses np.exp(entropy)/ent.exp()). (3) No misread: the cited lines read exactly as the comparing agent stated. A V3-vs-V2 "effective rank" comparison is therefore not apples-to-apples. The divergent relationship holds.

verifiedHighPoints.topic: Belief UMAP figure approach: taxonomy-colored single embedding vs geometry-faithful distance-matrix clustering
verifiedHighPoints.v3: vfe3/viz/figures.py:672-712 plot_belief_umap — umap_embed:57 of mu (Euclidean) / sigma (log-Euclidean vech) / phi, ONE embedding colored by BPE + function/content taxonomy (_bpe_category:583, _funccontent_category:604), native-space silhouette+CH in title
verifiedHighPoints.v2: semantic_clustering: geometry.py builds precomputed Bhattacharyya/log-Euclidean/Omega-geodesic distance matrices, projection.py:35 project (UMAP precomputed -> tSNE -> MDS), clustering.py:72 agglomerative auto-k, plotting.py:322-427 four scatters colored by cluster label
verifiedHighPoints.implication: Both produce per-token belief UMAPs over (mu, sigma, phi), but the pipelines diverge fundamentally: V3 embeds in a Euclidean/log-Euclidean feature chart and overlays a SUPERVISED linguistic taxonomy; V2 embeds geometry-faithful DIVERGENCE distances (Bhattacharyya for Sigma, GL+ geodesic for Omega) and clusters UNSUPERVISED. V3 drops the Omega-geodesic view and the non-Euclidean Sigma distance; V2 lacks the linguistic-category coloring.

verdict.topic: Belief UMAP figure approach: taxonomy-colored single embedding vs geometry-faithful distance-matrix clustering
verdict.evidence_v3: vfe3/viz/figures.py:698 "coords = umap_embed(feats, seed=seed)" over _belief_channel_features:380-391 (mu direct / sigma log-Euclidean vech / phi gauge coords); colored by supervised taxonomy figures.py:702 "labels, names = (None, None) if decode is None else _token_category_labels(token_ids, decode, tax)" with _funccontent_category:620 "return 2 if core in _STOPWORDS else 3". Grep of vfe3/viz for precomputed|bhattacharyya|agglomerative|dissimilarity|HDBSCAN|fit_predict: zero hits. The only geodesic in V3 is metrics.py:233 spd_geodesic_distance, used in extract.py:267 for sequential across-training Sigma drift — NOT a pairwise belief distance matrix and NOT an Omega/GL+(K) geodesic.
verdict.evidence_v2: geometry.py:172 logeuclidean Frobenius distance matrix, :198 _bhattacharyya_diag, :314 omega_geodesic_distances "d_{ij,h} = || log(Omega_{h,i}^{-1} Omega_{h,j}) ||_F" on GL+(d_h); projection.py:130 "reducer = umap.UMAP(metric=\"precomputed\", ...)" with t-SNE/MDS fallback; clustering.py:55 "AgglomerativeClustering(metric=\"precomputed\", linkage=\"average\", n_clusters=k)" swept for silhouette-max auto-k (:62-65); plotting.py:254 "label=f\"cluster {int(lab)}\"" — colored by UNSUPERVISED cluster id across four scatters plot_mu/sigma/phi/omega_clustering (:322,:349,:375,:403). Grep of semantic_clustering for stopword|function_content|taxonomy|linguistic: zero hits.
verdict.finding: The divergent relationship survives the adversarial attack on both directions. (1) V3's "missing" geometry-faithful pieces do not exist under another name in its viz module: no precomputed-distance UMAP, no Bhattacharyya Sigma distance, no Omega/GL+(K) geodesic distance matrix, no unsupervised agglomerative/HDBSCAN clustering (zero grep hits). The superficially-tempting V3 spd_geodesic_distance (metrics.py:233) is a red herring — it is an affine-invariant SPD-CONE distance between successive Sigma snapshots along a training trajectory (extract.py:267), not a pairwise inter-token distance matrix and not the GL+(K) transport geodesic V2 builds; it never feeds UMAP or clustering. (2) V2's "missing" linguistic taxonomy genuinely does not exist in its semantic_clustering package: zero stopword/function_content/taxonomy hits; plotting.py colors strictly by cluster id (label=\"cluster {int(lab)}\"), with token_strings used only for sparse text annotation, never for color. (3) The shared substrate is real (both produce per-token belief embeddings over mu/Sigma/phi via UMAP), so this is divergence within a common purpose, not unrelated code. The one subtlety worth flagging under the high-significance label: V3 DOES retain a log-Euclidean Sigma representation (figures.py:386-391 vech(log Sigma)), so the claim's phrase 'V3 drops ... the non-Euclidean Sigma distance' is only half right — V3 keeps the log-Euclidean chart but as Euclidean-metric FEATURES, dropping only the Bhattacharyya divergence-distance and the precomputed-matrix treatment; V2 has BOTH logeuclidean and bhattacharyya. This nuance does not weaken the verdict; the pipelines diverge fundamentally (feature-chart Euclidean UMAP + supervised taxonomy vs divergence/geodesic precomputed-distance UMAP + unsupervised auto-k clustering), and the Omega-geodesic view is present only in V2.
